failure_modes.text: Organizations can promote excellence in one role into incompetence in the next by assuming the same skill set scales upward unchanged.
failure_modes.source_quote: promoting individuals based purely on their excellence in a current role rather than their competence and specific skillset for the next role inevitably leads to incompetence at higher levels
failure_modes.mitigation: Evaluate promotion readiness against the demands of the target role, not the current role: design assessments that test the skills the new position actually requires.

failure_modes.text: Leaders can misdiagnose a role-design or support problem as an individual character flaw.
failure_modes.source_quote: It helps separate incompetence, mismatch, and missing support rather than collapsing them into one judgment.
failure_modes.mitigation: When performance drops after promotion, diagnose the system first: check whether role design, support, training, or team structure is the bottleneck before attributing failure to the individual.

failure_modes.text: The label can become fatalism if nobody redesigns the role, support, or promotion criteria after naming the pattern.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When the Diagnosis Becomes Fatalism
failure_modes.mitigation: After naming the pattern, act on it: redesign the role, add coaching, create parallel career tracks, or adjust promotion criteria — the label without structural change is just fatalism.

premortem_questions.text: What specific skills does this next role require that the candidate has never had to use before?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What are the specific skills this individual has never had to use that are essential for this new role?

premortem_questions.text: If this promotion is a spectacular failure a year from now, what is more likely: bad fit, bad support, or bad role design?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this promotion is a spectacular failure a year from now, what specifically was the root cause

premortem_questions.text: Are we rewarding visible success in the current role because it is familiar, or because it predicts success in the next one?

heuristics.text: Assess the distinct skills of the next role directly instead of treating past excellence as sufficient proof.
heuristics.source_quote: the organization must assess the distinct skills required for each role

heuristics.text: Use complementary team design to cover capability gaps that do not have to sink the promotion.
heuristics.source_quote: find solutions such that their weaknesses do not matter

heuristics.text: Frame the promotion review around future-fit capability, not only around historical reward logic.
heuristics.source_quote: rewarding past performance without validating future-fit capability

curation_notes.summary: Focused on mismatch between past performance and future-fit capability rather than on fatalistic workplace satire.
donor_drops: Dropped broader donor-style elaboration in favor of three compact source-grounded intervention chunks per type.
open_questions: Relation richness stays in Wave 3; Wave 2 only preserves the intervention surface needed for preview substrate use.

allies: Wave 3 preserves the first-order ally surface where the raw source gives a clean current-vocabulary seam.
antagonists: Wave 3 preserves the strongest current-vocabulary conflicts and leaves weaker cross-model seams omitted on purpose.
structured_tensions: Wave 4 remains observational only; this batch does not widen higher-order source coverage.
